options.help: Time interval for optimization calculations. '1hour' for hourly optimization, '15min' for quarter-hourly optimization (more detailed, higher computation).
When enabled, scheduled tasks will run automatically at configured times. Disable to prevent all scheduled tasks from running.
Define when tasks should run. Add entries with time patterns (e.g., '0435', 'xx00') and actions.

options.description: Period in days for baseload calculation

options.helpText: Baseload power consumption in watts for each hour of the day (24 values). Leave empty to use calculated baseload.

options.help: Meteoserver API access key. Get from Meteoserver.nl account. Use !secret for security. Required for weather forecasts.

options.description: Latitude (auto-fetched from HA if not set)
Longitude (auto-fetched from HA if not set)
Timezone (auto-fetched from HA if not set)
Country code (auto-fetched from HA if not set)

options.help: Home Assistant IP address or hostname. Usually auto-detected when running as HA add-on. Set manually only if auto-detection fails. Examples: '192.168.1.100', 'homeassistant.local'.
options.description: Home Assistant IP address (auto-detected if not set)

options.help: Home Assistant web interface port. Default is 8123. Change only if using custom port. Usually auto-detected when running as add-on.
Long-lived access token for HA API. Usually auto-provided when running as add-on. Create manually: Profile → Security → Long-Lived Access Tokens. Use !secret for security.
options.description: Home Assistant long-lived access token (can use !secret)
options.validationHint: Auto-provided as add-on, use !secret if manual

options.help: Protocol for Home Assistant API. 'http' for local access, 'https' for SSL/TLS. Usually auto-detected. Set manually only if needed.

options.helpText:
# Electric Vehicle Configuration

Optimize EV charging based on electricity prices, solar production, and charging deadlines.

## Charging Modes

### Instant Charging
Use `entity_instant_start` + `entity_instant_level` for immediate charging:
- Trigger instant_start to charge now
- System charges to instant_level immediately
- Simpler but less cost-optimized

### Scheduled Charging
Use `charge_scheduler` for time-based optimization:
- Set target level and ready datetime
- System optimizes when to charge for lowest cost
- Ensures vehicle is ready by deadline
- More complex but better cost optimization

## Requirements
- Must configure EITHER instant charging OR scheduler (not both)
- Vehicle must be home (device tracker)
- Vehicle must be plugged in (binary sensor)
- Charger must support amperage control

## Tips
- Use multiple charge stages for better efficiency modeling
- Three-phase charging (11/22kW) much faster than single-phase (3.7/7.4kW)
- Consider time-of-use tariffs when setting charge deadlines

options.help: Unique name for this electric vehicle. Use descriptive names like 'Tesla Model 3' or 'Polestar 2' for multiple vehicles.
Usable battery capacity in kilowatt-hours. Check vehicle specifications (often less than advertised total capacity).
Home Assistant device tracker entity to detect if vehicle is home. Charging is only scheduled when vehicle is at home location.
options.description: HA device tracker for vehicle position

options.help: True for three-phase charging (11kW/22kW), False for single-phase (3.7kW/7.4kW). Can also be HA entity ID for dynamic resolution.
options.description: Whether vehicle charges on three phases

options.help: Charging curve defined by amperage stages and their efficiencies. At least one stage required. Multiple stages enable optimization of charging speed.
Home Assistant sensor showing current battery State of Charge in percent. Required for charge optimization.
options.description: HA entity for current battery level %

options.help: Home Assistant binary sensor indicating if vehicle is plugged into charger. Charging is only possible when plugged in.
options.description: HA binary sensor for plugged in status

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to trigger immediate charging. Use with entity_instant_level for instant charging mode. Alternative to charge_scheduler.
options.description: HA entity for instant start charging

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity for instant charge target level. When instant_start triggers, system charges to this level immediately. Alternative to charge_scheduler.
options.description: HA entity for instant charge level target

options.help: Optional: Advanced scheduler for time-based charging. Optimizes when to charge to meet deadline at lowest cost. Alternative to instant charging.
Home Assistant switch entity to start/stop charging. System will control this to execute optimized charging schedule.
options.description: HA switch entity to control charging

options.help: Home Assistant entity to control charging current in amperes. System will adjust this to optimize charging speed and cost.
options.description: HA entity to set charging amperage

options.help: Home Assistant datetime entity specifying when to stop charging. Provides manual override of optimized schedule.
options.description: HA entity for stop charging datetime

options.helpText:
# Appliance / Machine Configuration

Optimize operation of flexible load appliances (washing machines, dishwashers, dryers, etc.) based on electricity prices and constraints.

## How It Works

The system optimizes when to start appliances within time windows:
1. User sets start window (earliest start) and end window (must finish by)
2. User selects program (each program has a power profile)
3. System calculates optimal start time to minimize cost
4. System writes calculated start time to HA entity
5. User/automation triggers machine at optimal time

## Programs

Each program defines power consumption over time:
- **Power profile**: List of watts per time interval
- **Duration**: Length of power list (e.g., 5 values = 5 hours)
- **'off' program**: Required, with zero power consumption

Examples:
- Eco wash: `[1800, 1800, 400, 400, 100]` (5 hours)
- Quick wash: `[2200, 500, 100]` (3 hours)
- Off: `[]` or `[0]`

## Time Windows

- **Start window**: Earliest allowed start (e.g., "now" or "20:00")
- **End window**: Latest allowed finish (e.g., "08:00 next day")
- System finds cheapest start time within window
- Machine must complete before end window

## Tips

- Define accurate power profiles for each program
- Set realistic time windows (at least program duration)
- Wider time windows = better optimization opportunities
- Combine with solar production forecasts
- Use instant start for urgent loads
- Multiple machines can be optimized together

# Appliance / Machine Configuration

Optimize operation of flexible load appliances (washing machines, dishwashers, dryers, etc.) based on electricity prices and constraints.

## How It Works

The system optimizes when to start appliances within time windows:
1. User sets start window (earliest start) and end window (must finish by)
2. User selects program (each program has a power profile)
3. System calculates optimal start time to minimize cost
4. System writes calculated start time to HA entity
5. User/automation triggers machine at optimal time

## Programs

Each program defines power consumption over time:
- **Power profile**: List of watts per time interval
- **Duration**: Length of power list (e.g., 5 values = 5 hours)
- **'off' program**: Required, with zero power consumption

Examples:
- Eco wash: `[1800, 1800, 400, 400, 100]` (5 hours)
- Quick wash: `[2200, 500, 100]` (3 hours)
- Off: `[]` or `[0]`

## Time Windows

- **Start window**: Earliest allowed start (e.g., "now" or "20:00")
- **End window**: Latest allowed finish (e.g., "08:00 next day")
- System finds cheapest start time within window
- Machine must complete before end window

## Tips

- Define accurate power profiles for each program
- Set realistic time windows (at least program duration)
- Wider time windows = better optimization opportunities
- Combine with solar production forecasts
- Use instant start for urgent loads
- Multiple machines can be optimized together

options.help: Unique name for this appliance. Use descriptive names like 'Washing Machine', 'Dishwasher', or 'Dryer' for multiple machines.
List of available programs with their power profiles. Must include at least one program. Always include an 'off' program with zero power consumption.
options.validationHint: At least 1 program required, include 'off' program

options.help: Home Assistant datetime entity for earliest allowed start time. Machine can start any time after this. Example: 'Now' or '18:00 today'.
options.description: HA entity for start window datetime

options.help: Home Assistant datetime entity for latest allowed completion time. Machine must finish before this deadline. Example: '08:00 tomorrow'.
options.description: HA entity for end window datetime

options.help: Home Assistant entity to select which program to run. Must match program names defined in 'programs' list.
Home Assistant entity where system writes the calculated optimal start time. User/automation can use this to trigger machine.
options.description: HA entity for calculated optimal start time

options.help: Home Assistant entity where system writes the calculated program end time. Useful for notifications and planning.
options.description: HA entity for calculated end time

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to force immediate start, bypassing optimization. Useful for urgent wash cycles.

options.helpText:
# Battery Configuration

Configure your home battery storage system for optimal energy management and cost savings.

## Key Settings
- **Capacity**: Total storage capacity in kWh
- **SOC Limits**: Upper and lower charge limits to protect battery health
- **Power Stages**: Define charging/discharging efficiency curves
- **Control Entities**: Home Assistant entities to control the battery

## Tips
- Set upper_limit to 80-90% for longer battery life
- Configure charge/discharge stages for accurate optimization
- Monitor battery degradation with cycle_cost setting

# Battery Configuration

Configure your home battery storage system for optimal energy management and cost savings.

## Key Settings
- **Capacity**: Total storage capacity in kWh
- **SOC Limits**: Upper and lower charge limits to protect battery health
- **Power Stages**: Define charging/discharging efficiency curves
- **Control Entities**: Home Assistant entities to control the battery

## Tips
- Set upper_limit to 80-90% for longer battery life
- Configure charge/discharge stages for accurate optimization
- Monitor battery degradation with cycle_cost setting

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor for calculated State of Charge. System can compute SOC from power flows if BMS sensor is unavailable.
Unique name to identify this battery in logs and reports. Use a descriptive name like 'Home Battery' or 'Garage Battery'.
Home Assistant entity that reports the current State of Charge (SOC) percentage. Usually a sensor from your battery inverter.
options.description: HA entity for current battery SOC

options.help: Total usable energy storage capacity of your battery. Check your battery specifications. For multiple batteries, sum their capacities.
Maximum State of Charge in percent. Battery will never charge above this level. Supports FlexValue pattern: use integer or HA entity ID.
Minimum State of Charge in percent. Battery will never discharge below this level. Supports FlexValue pattern: use integer or HA entity ID.
Target SOC level for cost optimization. System will prefer this level over minimum. Supports FlexValue pattern.
options.description: Optimal lower SOC % for cost optimization

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity specifying minimum battery level required at end of optimization window. Useful for ensuring battery charge overnight.
options.description: HA entity for minimum SOC at end of optimization period

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity specifying maximum battery level at end of optimization window. Rarely needed but available for advanced scenarios.
options.description: HA entity for maximum SOC at end of optimization period

options.help: Power stages for charging with corresponding efficiencies. Defines charge curve from AC grid to battery. At least one stage required.
options.validationHint: At least 1 stage required, ordered by power

options.help: Power stages for discharging with corresponding efficiencies. Defines discharge curve from battery to AC grid. At least one stage required.
options.validationHint: At least 1 stage required, ordered by power

options.help: Optional: Restrict battery power during specific hours. Example: {'22': 1000, '23': 1000} limits to 1000W from 22:00-23:59. Useful for noise reduction at night.
options.description: Hour -> max power mapping for reduced power hours
options.validationHint: Keys are hour strings (0-23), values are watts

options.help: Minimum power threshold in watts. Operations below this power level will be rounded to zero. Prevents inefficient micro-charging/discharging.
Efficiency of DC-coupled solar to battery conversion. Typically 0.95-0.98 for modern DC-coupled systems. Only relevant if DC-coupled solar is configured.
Maximum power for DC-coupled solar charging in watts. Determines how much DC solar power can flow directly to battery.
options.description: DC to battery max power in watts

options.help: Efficiency of battery to DC bus conversion. Typically 0.95-0.98. Only relevant for DC-coupled loads or reverse DC flow scenarios.
Maximum power for battery to DC bus conversion in watts. Rarely used in typical residential setups.
options.description: Battery to DC max power in watts

options.help: Degradation cost per full charge-discharge cycle in euros. Used to factor battery wear into optimization. Calculate as: (battery_cost / warranted_cycles). Example: €5000 battery with 6000 cycles = €0.83/cycle.
options.description: Cost per battery cycle in euros

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to control grid feed-in power. Used by scheduler to execute optimized battery operations.
options.description: HA entity to set power feed-in to grid

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to control battery operating mode (e.g., auto/manual/off). System will switch modes as needed for optimization.
options.description: HA entity to set battery operating mode

options.help: Value to send to operating mode entity for 'ON' state. Example: 'auto', 'enabled', 'on'. Must match actual entity values.
Value to send to operating mode entity for 'OFF' state. Example: 'manual', 'disabled', 'off'. Must match actual entity values.
Optional: Home Assistant entity to emergency stop the battery inverter. Rarely needed but available for safety scenarios.
Optional: Home Assistant entity to enable/disable grid balancing mode. Used for frequency regulation participation or grid services.
options.description: HA entity for grid balancing switch

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current power flow from battery in watts. Used for monitoring and validation.
options.description: HA entity for power from battery

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current DC-coupled solar power in watts. Only relevant for DC-coupled solar installations.
options.description: HA entity for power from PV

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current AC grid power flow in watts. Used for monitoring overall system balance.
options.description: HA entity for power from AC

options.help: Optional: Configure DC-coupled solar panels directly connected to this battery inverter. DC coupling is more efficient than AC coupling. Leave empty for AC-coupled or grid-only batteries.
options.description: DC-coupled solar panels attached to this battery

options.helpText:
# Solar Panel Configuration

Configure your solar panel installations for accurate production forecasting.

## Configuration Options

### Simple Configuration
For panels all facing the same direction, use the flat config:
- **tilt**: Panel angle (0-90°)
- **orientation**: Direction panels face (0=south)
- **capacity**: Total capacity in kWp
- **yield**: Efficiency factor (0.8-0.9)

### Advanced Configuration (Multiple Strings)
For panels facing different directions, use the 'strings' configuration:
- Each string can have different tilt and orientation
- Allows accurate modeling of complex installations
- Example: Panels on both south and east-facing roofs

## Tips
- Use strings configuration for multi-directional installations
- Include all inverter and cable losses in yield factor
- Account for shading in yield factor

# Solar Panel Configuration

Configure your solar panel installations for accurate production forecasting.

## Configuration Options

### Simple Configuration
For panels all facing the same direction, use the flat config:
- **tilt**: Panel angle (0-90°)
- **orientation**: Direction panels face (0=south)
- **capacity**: Total capacity in kWp
- **yield**: Efficiency factor (0.8-0.9)

### Advanced Configuration (Multiple Strings)
For panels facing different directions, use the 'strings' configuration:
- Each string can have different tilt and orientation
- Allows accurate modeling of complex installations
- Example: Panels on both south and east-facing roofs

## Tips
- Use strings configuration for multi-directional installations
- Include all inverter and cable losses in yield factor
- Account for shading in yield factor

options.help: Unique name for this solar installation. Use descriptive names like 'Roof South' or 'Garage East' for multiple installations.
Optional Home Assistant entity to enable or disable this solar installation in the optimization. Useful for maintenance or testing.
options.description: HA entity to enable/disable this solar installation

options.help: Panel tilt angle for simple configuration. Use this OR 'strings', not both. Leave empty if using strings configuration.
options.validationHint: 0-90 degrees, leave empty when using strings

options.help: Panel orientation for simple configuration. Use this OR 'strings', not both. Leave empty if using strings configuration.
options.validationHint: -180 to 180 degrees, leave empty when using strings

options.help: Total capacity for simple configuration. Use this OR 'strings', not both. Leave empty if using strings configuration.
options.validationHint: Greater than 0, leave empty when using strings

options.help: Yield factor for simple configuration. Use this OR 'strings', not both. Leave empty if using strings configuration.
options.validationHint: Greater than 0, typically 0.8-0.9, leave empty when using strings

options.help: Advanced: Configure multiple strings for panels with different orientations or tilts. Use this OR flat config (tilt/orientation/capacity/yield), not both.
options.description: Multiple panel strings with different configurations

options.help: Maximum power available from grid connection in kilowatts. Based on your main fuse/circuit breaker rating. Typical residential: 1-phase=7.4kW (32A), 3-phase=17kW (25A) or 25kW (35A). Prevents optimization from exceeding grid capacity.
options.validationHint: Must be > 0, typical 7-25 kW for residential

options.help: Enable hot water boiler optimization. Set to true to include boiler in optimization, false to disable. Can also be HA entity ID for dynamic control.
Home Assistant sensor showing current hot water temperature in °C. Required for thermal state tracking and heating decisions.
options.description: HA entity for actual water temperature

options.help: Home Assistant entity for target water temperature. System will heat water to this temperature. Typical: 55-65°C for domestic hot water.
Home Assistant entity for temperature hysteresis in °C. Prevents excessive cycling. If setpoint=60°C and hysteresis=5°C, heating starts at 55°C.
Optional: Home Assistant binary sensor indicating if boiler is enabled. System will only optimize when boiler is enabled.
options.description: HA entity for boiler enabled status

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to trigger immediate boiler heating. Overrides optimized schedule for on-demand hot water.
Coefficient of Performance if using heat pump water heater. For resistive heating element, use 1.0. For heat pump water heater, typically 2.5-4.0.
options.validationHint: Must be > 0, use 1.0 for resistive, 2.5-4.0 for heat pump

options.help: Rate at which water temperature drops when not heating, in °C per hour. Depends on insulation quality. Typical: 0.5-2.0°C/h for well-insulated boilers.
options.description: Cooling rate in degrees per hour

options.help: Hot water tank volume in liters. Affects thermal inertia and heating time. Typical residential: 100-300 liters.
Maximum temperature for starting heating cycle. Heating only occurs when water is below this temperature. Typically same as or slightly above setpoint.
options.description: Temperature below which heating is allowed

options.help: Electrical power consumption of heating element or heat pump compressor in watts. Typical: 1000-3000W for resistive, 400-800W for heat pump water heater.
Home Assistant service type to trigger boiler heating. Use 'press' for button entities, 'switch' for switch entities, or custom service names.
Home Assistant entity used to activate boiler heating. System will trigger this entity using activate_service when heating is needed.
Enable heating system optimization. Set to true to include heat pump in optimization, false to disable. Can also be HA entity ID for dynamic control.
Optional: Home Assistant binary sensor indicating if heat pump is enabled and operational. System will only optimize when enabled.
options.description: HA binary sensor for heat pump enabled status

options.help: Multiplier for degree-day heat demand calculation. Adjust based on building insulation and heat loss. Higher = more heat needed. Typical: 0.5-2.0.
options.description: Degree days factor for heat demand calculation

options.help: Heat pump control mode: 'on/off' = simple binary control, 'power' = variable power control, 'heating curve' = adjust heating curve based on weather.
Power and efficiency stages for heat pump. At least one stage required. Multiple stages model variable-speed compressors. Must be sorted by power ascending.
options.validationHint: At least 1 stage, must be sorted by max_power

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant entity to adjust heating curve. Used when adjustment mode is 'heating curve'. Controls water temperature based on outdoor temperature.
options.description: HA entity to adjust heating curve

options.help: Optional: Multiplier for heating curve adjustments. Higher values = more aggressive adjustments. Typical: 0.5-2.0.
Minimum number of consecutive time intervals heat pump must run once started. Prevents excessive on/off cycling which reduces efficiency and equipment life. Typical: 2-4 intervals (2-4 hours).
options.description: Minimum run length in time intervals

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing total heat energy produced. Used for monitoring and validation.
Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current heat demand. Can be used instead of degree-day calculation for more accurate demand forecasting.
Optional: Home Assistant sensor for outdoor average temperature. Used for degree-day calculations and COP adjustments.
Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current COP. Can be used for monitoring or to override stage-based COP calculations.
options.description: HA entity for heat pump COP

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant sensor showing current electrical power consumption. Used for monitoring and validation.
options.description: HA entity for heat pump power

options.help: Optional: Home Assistant switch to control heat pump on/off. Used by scheduler to execute optimized heating schedule.
options.description: HA entity to control heat pump on/off

options.help: Database engine for Day Ahead optimizer data. SQLite is simplest (no server needed), MySQL/PostgreSQL for advanced setups or shared databases.
Directory path for SQLite database file. Relative to add-on root. Example: '../data' stores in persistent data folder. Only for SQLite.
options.validationHint: Required for SQLite (or use database field)

options.help: For SQLite: filename (e.g., 'day_ahead.db'). For MySQL/PostgreSQL: database name. At least one of db_path or database required for SQLite.
options.description: Database filename for SQLite or database name for MySQL
options.validationHint: Filename for SQLite, database name for MySQL/PostgreSQL

options.help: Hostname or IP of MySQL/PostgreSQL server. Required for server-based engines. Examples: 'localhost', '192.168.1.100'.
Database server port. Required for MySQL/PostgreSQL. Standard ports: 3306 (MySQL), 5432 (PostgreSQL).
Database username for authentication. Required for MySQL/PostgreSQL.
Database password. Use secrets.json with '!secret password_key' for security. Never store passwords directly in config.
Optional: Timezone for database timestamps. Examples: 'Europe/Amsterdam', 'UTC'. Usually not needed if database and system timezones match.
Port number for Day Ahead Optimizer web dashboard. Access at http://homeassistant.local:PORT. Must be between 1024-65535 (non-privileged ports).
Database engine where Home Assistant stores history data. Most HA installations use SQLite, but MySQL/MariaDB and PostgreSQL are also supported.
Hostname or IP address of database server. Required for MySQL/PostgreSQL, not used for SQLite. Examples: 'localhost', '192.168.1.100', 'mysql.local'.
Database server port. If not specified, defaults to 3306 for MySQL or 5432 for PostgreSQL. Not used for SQLite.
Name of the Home Assistant database. Default 'homeassistant' matches standard HA installation.
Username for database authentication. Default 'homeassistant' matches standard HA installation.
Database password. Use secrets.json with '!secret password_key' pattern for security. Never store passwords directly in config.
Optional: Home Assistant notification service entity. Used to send notifications about optimization events. Example: 'notify.mobile_app' or 'notify.persistent_notification'.
Send notification when Day Ahead Optimizer starts up. Useful for monitoring add-on status. Can be boolean or HA entity ID.
Send notification when optimization calculation completes. Includes summary of results (costs, battery schedule, etc.). Can be boolean or HA entity ID.
Optional: Home Assistant entity to update with last activity timestamp. Useful for monitoring and automations. Example: 'input_datetime.dao_last_run'.
options.description: HA entity to track last activity timestamp

options.help: Tibber API access token. Get from Tibber app or developer portal. Use !secret for security. Required if using Tibber as price source.
Tibber GraphQL API endpoint URL. Default is official API. Change only for testing or custom endpoints.
Supplier markup/fees for consumption (excluding VAT) indexed by effective date. Fixed part of electricity cost. Format: {'2024-01-01': 0.02}.
options.description: Supplier costs for consumption by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> euro/kWh ex VAT)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, float values (ex VAT)

options.help: Supplier fees for feed-in/production (excluding VAT) indexed by effective date. May be negative (credit). Format: {'2024-01-01': -0.02}.
options.description: Supplier costs for production by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> euro/kWh ex VAT)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, float values (ex VAT)

options.help: Data source for day-ahead electricity market prices. 'nordpool' for Nordic/Baltic, 'entsoe' for European markets, 'tibber' if using Tibber integration.
API key for ENTSO-E Transparency Platform. Required if source_day_ahead='entsoe'. Get free key at transparency.entsoe.eu. Use !secret for security.
Date of last electricity invoice. Used for calculating costs since last billing period. Format: YYYY-MM-DD. Update after receiving invoices.
Energy taxes on consumption (excluding VAT) indexed by effective date. Format: {'2024-01-01': 0.05}. Use date when tariff changes.
options.description: Energy taxes for consumption by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> euro/kWh ex VAT)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, float values (ex VAT)

options.help: Energy taxes on feed-in/production (excluding VAT) indexed by effective date. Often zero or negative. Format: {'2024-01-01': 0.0}.
options.description: Energy taxes for production by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> euro/kWh ex VAT)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, float values (ex VAT)

options.help: VAT percentage on consumption indexed by effective date. Format: {'2024-01-01': 21}. Applied to market price + taxes + supplier costs.
options.description: VAT percentage for consumption by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> %)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, integer 0-100 values

options.help: VAT percentage on feed-in/production indexed by effective date. Format: {'2024-01-01': 21}. Often same as consumption VAT.
options.description: VAT percentage for production by date (YYYY-MM-DD -> %)
options.validationHint: Dict with YYYY-MM-DD keys, integer 0-100 values

options.help: Enable tax refund calculation if eligible. Some regions/users get energy tax refunds for solar production. Can be boolean or HA entity ID.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring grid consumption (import). Used for actual vs optimized comparison and reporting.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring grid production (export/feed-in). Used for production reporting and solar surplus calculation.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities for AC-coupled solar production. After inverter, directly to AC side. Separate from DC-coupled solar.
options.description: HA entities for AC solar production

options.help: List of Home Assistant sensor entities for DC-coupled solar production. Before inverter, directly to battery DC bus. Separate from AC-coupled solar.
options.description: HA entities for DC solar production

options.help: List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring EV charging consumption. Used for EV-specific energy reporting and cost allocation.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring heat pump consumption. Used for heating-specific energy reporting and COP calculation.
options.description: HA entities for heat pump (warmtepomp) consumption

options.help: List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring hot water boiler consumption. Used for boiler-specific energy reporting.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring battery charging (consumption). Used for battery efficiency and cycling cost calculation.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring battery discharging (production). Used for battery efficiency and round-trip calculation.
List of Home Assistant sensor entities measuring appliance/machine consumption (washing machine, dishwasher, etc.). Used for machine-specific reporting.
Optional: Home Assistant sensor for grid CO2 intensity (gCO2/kWh). Used to calculate and report carbon footprint of electricity usage.
Optional: Additional custom sensor configurations. Advanced use for extending reporting capabilities beyond standard entities.
Number of days to retain optimization history in database. Older data is automatically cleaned up. Longer retention enables better trend analysis but increases database size. Minimum 1 day.
options.description: Number of days to retain historical data

options.help: Matplotlib visual style for generated graphs. 'dark_background' matches Home Assistant dark theme. Other options: 'default', 'seaborn', 'ggplot', 'bmh', 'fivethirtyeight'.
Enable graph generation and display. Graphs show optimization results, prices, battery schedules. Set to true to enable, false to disable. Can be HA entity ID.
Display battery state of charge and power flows in graphs. Shows charge/discharge schedule over time.
Display consumption prices (market + taxes + VAT) in graphs. Helps visualize expensive vs cheap periods.
Display feed-in/production prices in graphs. Useful if you have solar feed-in to compare consumption vs production value.
Display raw day-ahead spot market prices (before taxes/markup) in graphs. Shows pure market price variations.
Display average/baseline consumption in graphs. Helps understand optimization impact relative to normal usage.
